m365-178.attackPerspective: Unmanaged devices accessing corporate resources are blind spots — no compliance checks, no patching enforcement, no remote wipe capability. Attackers target users who access corporate data from personal devices where there is no EDR, no disk encryption verification, and no conditional access enforcement. A single unmanaged device with cached credentials can be the entry point for a full breach.
m365-178.implementationNotes:
# Connect to Microsoft Graph for Intune
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'DeviceManagementManagedDevices.Read.All','DeviceManagementServiceConfig.ReadWrite.All'

# Get all managed devices
$devices = Get-MgDeviceManagementManagedDevice -All
Write-Host "Total managed devices: $($devices.Count)"

# Find non-compliant devices
$nonCompliant = $devices | Where-Object { $_.ComplianceState -ne 'compliant' }
Write-Host "Non-compliant: $($nonCompliant.Count)"
$nonCompliant | Select-Object DeviceName, UserPrincipalName, ComplianceState, OSVersion, LastSyncDateTime | Format-Table

# Get devices not synced in 30+ days (stale)
$stale = $devices | Where-Object { $_.LastSyncDateTime -lt (Get-Date).AddDays(-30) }
Write-Host "Stale (30+ days): $($stale.Count)"
$stale | Select-Object DeviceName, UserPrincipalName, LastSyncDateTime | Format-Table
m365-178.validationNotes:
# Verify enrollment restrictions are configured
Get-MgDeviceManagementDeviceEnrollmentConfiguration | Select DisplayName, DeviceEnrollmentConfigurationType | Format-Table

# Verify compliance policies exist
Get-MgDeviceManagementDeviceCompliancePolicy | Select DisplayName, LastModifiedDateTime | Format-Table
# Expected: At least one policy per OS platform
m365-178.tuningNotes: Start with a 'report-only' compliance policy before enforcing to understand the impact. Common initial non-compliance causes: missing BitLocker, outdated OS, no screen lock. Allow a 24-72 hour grace period for users to remediate before blocking access. Exempt shared/kiosk devices from user-based enrollment.
m365-178.advancedDetection:
// KQL: Detect sign-ins from unmanaged devices
SigninLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(24h)
| where DeviceDetail.isManaged != true
| where AppDisplayName in ('Microsoft Teams', 'SharePoint Online', 'Exchange Online')
| summarize Count = count(), Apps = make_set(AppDisplayName) by UserPrincipalName, DeviceDetail.operatingSystem, tostring(DeviceDetail.browser)
| where Count > 5
| sort by Count desc

m365-182.attackPerspective: Compliance policies without Conditional Access enforcement are merely advisory — devices can be flagged non-compliant but still access all resources. Attackers love environments where compliance is configured but not enforced, because the security team has false confidence while the actual access controls are open.
m365-182.implementationNotes:
# Create a Windows compliance policy (example)
$compliancePolicy = @{
  displayName = 'SCW-Win-Compliance-Baseline'
  description = 'Baseline compliance for Windows devices'
  osMinimumVersion = '10.0.19045'  # Win 10 22H2+
  bitLockerEnabled = $true
  secureBootEnabled = $true
  codeIntegrityEnabled = $true
  firewallEnabled = $true
  antivirusRequired = $true
  passwordRequired = $true
  passwordMinimumLength = 12
  scheduledActionsForRule = @(@{
    ruleName = 'PasswordRequired'
    scheduledActionConfigurations = @(@{
      actionType = 'block'
      gracePeriodHours = 72
      notificationTemplateId = ''
    })
  })
}

# Link to Conditional Access
# Create CA policy: Require device compliance for all cloud apps
# Target: All users, Exclude: Break-glass accounts
# Grant: Require device to be marked as compliant
m365-182.tuningNotes: Start enforcement with a pilot group before expanding to all users. The 72-hour grace period is recommended for initial rollout — reduce to 24 hours after stabilization. Monitor the Intune reports for common non-compliance reasons and address the root cause (e.g., push BitLocker policy rather than just flagging it).
m365-182.advancedDetection:
// KQL: Detect non-compliant devices accessing sensitive apps
SigninLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(24h)
| where DeviceDetail.isCompliant != true
| where ResourceDisplayName in ('Microsoft Teams', 'SharePoint Online', 'Exchange Online')
| summarize AccessCount = count() by UserPrincipalName, tostring(DeviceDetail.displayName), ResourceDisplayName
| sort by AccessCount desc
